name: Cross Product
description: Multiply two vectors and get a vector perpendicular to both—useful for rotations and torque

content.overview: The cross product A × B gives a vector perpendicular to both A and B. Its magnitude tells you how perpendicular they are. {{viz:diagram-01}}
content.explanation:
Unlike the dot product (which gives a scalar), the cross product gives a VECTOR. {{viz:diagram-02}}

**Formula: Using Components**
If A = (Aₓ, Aᵧ, Aᵤ) and B = (Bₓ, Bᵧ, Bᵤ):

A × B = (Aᵧ×Bᵤ - Aᵤ×Bᵧ, Aᵤ×Bₓ - Aₓ×Bᵤ, Aₓ×Bᵧ - Aᵧ×Bₓ)

**Formula: Using Angle**
|A × B| = |A| × |B| × sin(θ)

The direction is perpendicular to both (use right-hand rule). {{viz:anim-01}}

**Right-Hand Rule:**
Point fingers in direction of A, curl toward B, thumb points in direction of A × B.

**What It Means:** {{viz:chart-01}}
- If θ = 0° (parallel): A × B = 0 (they don't create rotation)
- If θ = 90° (perpendicular): |A × B| = |A| × |B| (maximum effect)
- Order matters: A × B = -(B × A) (opposite direction)

**Real Physics:**

Torque = Position × Force (how much something spins)
Angular Momentum = Position × Momentum
Magnetic Force = Charge × (Velocity × Magnetic Field)

All use cross products!
keyPoints: A × B is a vector perpendicular to both A and B
Order matters: A × B ≠ B × A
Used for torque, angular momentum, magnetic force
content.whyItMatters: Anything involving rotation in physics uses the cross product. It's essential for understanding torque and angular quantities.
